A0:
**Recommendation: Server-side rendering (SSR), or better yet static site generation (SSG) — not client-side rendering (CSR).**

For a content-heavy marketing site, this isn't a close call. Here's the reasoning.

## Why not CSR

Client-side rendering ships a near-empty HTML shell plus a JS bundle, then renders content in the browser. For a marketing site that's the wrong trade in almost every dimension that matters:

- **SEO** — Marketing sites live or die on organic search. While Google can execute JS, its render budget is limited, indexing is delayed, and other crawlers (Bing, social scrapers, LLM crawlers) handle JS poorly or not at all. SSR/SSG gives every crawler fully-formed HTML immediately.
- **Social/link previews** — Open Graph and Twitter cards are read from the raw HTML by bots that *don't* run JS. CSR breaks these unless you bolt on extra infrastructure.
- **First paint / perceived speed** — CSR forces download → parse → execute → fetch data → render before anything useful appears. Marketing visitors are high-bounce and impatient; slow first paint costs conversions directly.
- **Core Web Vitals** — LCP and related metrics (which feed both ranking and real UX) are much easier to hit when content is in the initial HTML.

The classic CSR strength — rich app-like interactivity behind a login — is mostly irrelevant for marketing pages.

## SSG vs SSR — pick by content freshness

Both serve real HTML; the difference is *when* it's built:

| | Best when |
|---|---|
| **SSG** (build-time) | Content changes occasionally (most marketing/landing pages, blogs). Pages are pre-rendered to static files → fastest possible, cheapest to host, trivially CDN-cached. **This is my default for a marketing site.** |
| **SSR** (request-time) | Content is personalized, frequently updated, or pulled live from a CMS where build-time isn't fresh enough. |

A modern framework (Next.js, Astro, SvelteKit, Nuxt) lets you mix these per-route and add **ISR** (incremental static regeneration) — static speed with periodic background revalidation, so editors don't wait for a full rebuild.

## My concrete recommendation

1. **Default to SSG** for the bulk of the marketing pages.
2. **Use ISR / on-demand revalidation** if a CMS feeds the content, so edits go live without a manual deploy.
3. **Reserve SSR** for the few routes that are genuinely dynamic or personalized.
4. For a mostly-static, low-JS site, **Astro** is especially strong (ships zero JS by default, islands for interactivity); if you're already in the React ecosystem or expect more app-like features later, **Next.js** is the safe general choice.

If you tell me the content source (hardcoded vs. a CMS), update frequency, and whether you need any personalization, I can narrow this to a specific framework + rendering-mode-per-route setup.
A1s:
Use server-side rendering for a content-heavy marketing site — and more specifically, lean toward **static generation (SSG)** with small islands of client-side interactivity, rather than either pure server-per-request rendering or pure client-side rendering.

Here's the reasoning.

A marketing site's job is to be found and to convert, and that maps almost perfectly onto what server rendering does well:

- **SEO is the whole game.** Search and social crawlers get fully-formed HTML from a server-rendered page. Client-side rendering ships a near-empty shell plus a JS bundle and asks the crawler to execute it — which they do inconsistently and slowly. For a site whose traffic depends on organic discovery, that's a self-inflicted wound.
- **Speed drives conversion and ranking.** Server rendering gives a fast First/Largest Contentful Paint; CSR shows a blank screen until the bundle downloads, parses, and hydrates. Google's Core Web Vitals fold that delay directly into ranking, and bounce rate climbs with every second of blank screen.
- **Link previews need HTML.** Open Graph and Twitter-card meta tags must be present in the initial response for shares to render thumbnails and titles. CSR commonly breaks this.
- **Resilience.** The content still appears if JS is slow, blocked, or errors out — important when you don't control the visitor's network or device.

The refinement: marketing content changes infrequently, so you usually don't need to render on every request. **Pre-render at build time (SSG) and serve from a CDN** — it's faster, cheaper, and more resilient than per-request SSR, with the same SEO benefits. Reserve true per-request SSR for the genuinely dynamic or personalized pages (e.g. content that varies by user or A/B segment at request time). For the interactive pieces — forms, carousels, nav menus — hydrate just those parts as client-side "islands" instead of shipping JS for the whole page.

Practically, a framework like **Astro** fits this profile best because it ships zero JS by default and layers interactivity in selectively; **Next.js** is a fine choice too if you want one framework spanning the marketing site and a future app.

Client-side rendering only earns its place when SEO and first-load don't matter — a logged-in app dashboard, for instance. That's the opposite of a marketing site, so it's not the right default here.

Use server-side rendering for a content-heavy marketing site — and more specifically, lean toward **static generation (SSG)** with small islands of client-side interactivity, rather than either pure server-per-request rendering or pure client-side rendering.

Here's the reasoning.

A marketing site's job is to be found and to convert, and that maps almost perfectly onto what server rendering does well:

- **SEO is the whole game.** Search and social crawlers get fully-formed HTML from a server-rendered page. Client-side rendering ships a near-empty shell plus a JS bundle and asks the crawler to execute it — which they do inconsistently and slowly. For a site whose traffic depends on organic discovery, that's a self-inflicted wound.
- **Speed drives conversion and ranking.** Server rendering gives a fast First/Largest Contentful Paint; CSR shows a blank screen until the bundle downloads, parses, and hydrates. Google's Core Web Vitals fold that delay directly into ranking, and bounce rate climbs with every second of blank screen.
- **Link previews need HTML.** Open Graph and Twitter-card meta tags must be present in the initial response for shares to render thumbnails and titles. CSR commonly breaks this.
- **Resilience.** The content still appears if JS is slow, blocked, or errors out — important when you don't control the visitor's network or device.

The refinement: marketing content changes infrequently, so you usually don't need to render on every request. **Pre-render at build time (SSG) and serve from a CDN** — it's faster, cheaper, and more resilient than per-request SSR, with the same SEO benefits. Reserve true per-request SSR for the genuinely dynamic or personalized pages (e.g. content that varies by user or A/B segment at request time). For the interactive pieces — forms, carousels, nav menus — hydrate just those parts as client-side "islands" instead of shipping JS for the whole page.

Practically, a framework like **Astro** fits this profile best because it ships zero JS by default and layers interactivity in selectively; **Next.js** is a fine choice too if you want one framework spanning the marketing site and a future app.

Client-side rendering only earns its place when SEO and first-load don't matter — a logged-in app dashboard, for instance. That's the opposite of a marketing site, so it's not the right default here.

**What would change this recommendation:** if the "marketing site" is actually a gated, behind-login experience that search engines never see (e.g. an internal sales portal or a highly personalized per-user dashboard), the SEO and first-paint arguments lose their force and CSR or per-request SSR becomes reasonable. The other genuine limitation is build-time cost: pure SSG assumes content is known at build and changes on a publish cadence you can rebuild around — if the site has tens of thousands of pages or content that must go live the instant it's edited, you'll want incremental/on-demand regeneration or per-request SSR rather than a full static build. Short of those conditions, SSG-first stands.
